content.text: What It Is

Dark launch traffic routing is the practice of forwarding real production request traffic to a new or updated AI service while deliberately suppressing the service's responses from reaching end users. The system invokes the new agent, collects its outputs, and logs them alongside the production outputs for offline comparison — but only the existing production system's response is ever delivered to the caller.

The term "dark" means invisible to the user. From the user's perspective nothing has changed: they send a request and receive the expected response from the stable production system. In the background, however, the same request has already been dispatched to the new agent, which ran to completion, used its tools, and produced an output that engineers can examine at leisure.

For agentic AI this pattern is particularly important because agent behaviour is harder to characterise than classical software. An agent's latency, tool call count, cost, and output quality all depend on the live request distribution in ways that are impossible to reproduce fully in a staging environment. Dark launching routes representative production traffic through the new agent while keeping all risk to the user at zero, because their experience is backed entirely by the proven production system.
Dark launch traffic routing is distinct from two related concepts that are often confused with it:
Traffic mirroring (shadowing):

The router duplicates the live request and fires it asynchronously to the shadow service in parallel with the production dispatch. The production path is unaffected. The shadow path runs concurrently and its response is discarded after logging. This is the most common dark-launch implementation.
Traffic splitting (canary / A/B):
A percentage of users receive responses from the new system. Users are exposed to the new behaviour. This is NOT a dark launch; it is a canary or phased rollout.
Dark launch by user segment:

Internal employees or beta testers use the new system and see its live responses, but the general user population is unaffected. Strictly speaking this falls between a true dark launch and a canary, but "dark" is commonly used to describe it because no external users are exposed.
Why It Matters
Agentic systems fail in ways that are uniquely difficult to anticipate. A multi-step agent that successfully handles synthetic test cases can still fail badly on live traffic because:
Real user inputs contain edge cases, ambiguous language, and domain-specific phrasing that synthetic datasets rarely cover fully.
Tool call sequences are highly sensitive to small differences in the model's reasoning; a slightly different system prompt can produce a completely different tool chain for the same task.
Latency under production load is fundamentally different from latency under controlled test load — queuing effects, rate limits, and tool-service latency all interact.
Costs scale with production traffic patterns in ways that are hard to model in staging.
Multi-turn conversation agents accumulate state differently when exposed to real user intent distributions.

Dark launch routing gives you empirical signal from production traffic before any user is exposed to risk. The signal is not synthetic — it reflects the actual distribution of requests your users send. This is the only reliable way to validate an agentic system's behaviour at scale before committing to it as the production path.
Benefits
Zero user risk.
No user ever receives a response from the unvalidated agent. If the new agent panics, times out, or produces a hallucinated answer, the production system's response is served regardless.
Real traffic distribution.
You observe how the new agent behaves on the actual mix of queries your users send, not a curated sample. Edge cases that would never appear in a test suite appear naturally.
Side-by-side quality comparison.
Because both systems process the same request, you can build automated and manual comparison dashboards that align production and shadow outputs for the same input.
Cost and latency profiling at scale.
You measure actual token spend and p95/p99 latency under production load, not lab conditions.
Tool call instrumentation.
You can observe which tools the shadow agent calls, how many times, and in what order — revealing unexpected multi-step patterns that your unit tests did not cover.
Confidence before commitment.
The data gathered during dark launch becomes the evidence that justifies moving traffic to the new system. This gives non-engineering stakeholders an objective basis for approval.
Trade-offs
Double compute cost during the launch period.

Every dark-launched request is processed twice: once by production, once by the shadow. For LLM-heavy agents this can be a significant incremental cost. Managing it requires a sampling strategy (see Cost Management below).
Infrastructure complexity.
Traffic mirroring requires changes to the routing layer — a proxy, service mesh rule, or API gateway policy — and a shadow deployment that must be kept in sync with configuration changes.
Observability overhead.
Shadow traffic must be tagged, stored, and compared. You need pipelines to collect shadow responses, align them with production responses, and surface divergences.
Stateful sessions are harder.

Agentic multi-turn conversations cannot be trivially mirrored because the second turn of a shadow session depends on the first turn's tool results, which may differ from production. See the Stateful Routing section.
Tool side effects must be prevented.
If the shadow agent calls a tool that writes to a database, sends an email, or charges a payment, real harm occurs. Preventing tool side effects in shadow mode requires careful tool-level controls.
Lag in signal.
Because the shadow agent's output is never served to users, you only learn about failures through logs and metrics. Problems are silent until someone looks at the dashboards.
How It Works

The fundamental mechanism is traffic mirroring at the request routing layer. A request from a client arrives at a proxy or gateway. The proxy executes the production backend synchronously and returns its response to the client. In parallel (or with minimal delay), the proxy forwards an identical copy of the request to the shadow agent backend. The shadow backend processes the request — invoking the LLM, executing tool calls, completing the agent loop — and writes its result to a shadow log. The proxy discards the shadow response without surfacing it to the caller.

This sequence is important: the production path is on the critical path; the shadow path is not. If the shadow agent is slow or fails, the client is never affected. Timeouts and errors on the shadow path are logged but do not propagate.
The routing layer must make three decisions for every incoming request:
Should this request be dark-launched?
Based on routing policy — percentage sampling, header inspection, user segment, or cohort assignment.
How should the shadow copy be dispatched?

Asynchronously (fire-and-forget after production responds) or synchronously in parallel (both paths run concurrently and production response is returned immediately, shadow runs to completion in background).
What metadata should be attached to the shadow copy?
At minimum: a trace ID linking the shadow and production records, the routing policy that selected this request, and a dark-launch session label.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing three dark launch traffic routing patterns: header-based, percentage sampling, and cohort-based, plus isolation requirement categories

content.text: Architecture
Dark launch routing can be implemented at several architectural layers. The choice depends on your existing infrastructure:
API Gateway layer

(AWS API Gateway, Kong, Apigee): The gateway rule forwards a copy of selected requests to a shadow stage/service. Easiest to configure for stateless REST APIs. Less suitable for streaming or WebSocket-based agent protocols.
Reverse proxy
(nginx, HAProxy, Caddy): The mirror module duplicates requests at the TCP level, sending a copy to an upstream shadow server. The original request is proxied normally. Nginx's
mirror
directive is a common choice.
Service mesh
(Istio/Envoy): Traffic management rules apply mirroring at the sidecar proxy level. This works for any protocol the mesh understands (HTTP/1, HTTP/2, gRPC). Istio VirtualService objects support a
mirror
field that routes a copy of matched requests to a mirror host.
Application-level mirroring
: The production service itself forks the request asynchronously. This is the most portable approach but couples the production code to dark-launch logic, which must be carefully isolated.
Feature flag + dual dispatch

: A feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith) evaluates per-request targeting rules and returns a flag that tells the application whether to fire a shadow call. The application then calls both production and shadow agents, returns the production response, and asynchronously logs the shadow result.

In all cases the shadow backend is a separate deployment from production. It runs the new agent code, has its own resource quota, its own rate limits with the upstream LLM provider, and its own observability namespace. Sharing any of these with production would allow shadow traffic to starve production resources.
When to Use It
Dark launch traffic routing is the right tool when all of the following are true:
You are replacing or significantly changing an agent that handles real user requests.
The new agent's behaviour is difficult to fully validate in staging because it depends on open-ended user inputs.
A regression would harm users or incur significant support cost.
You have or can build the infrastructure to run a shadow backend without it affecting production.
It is not the right tool when:
The change is cosmetic or provably non-behavioural (e.g., only the system prompt formatting changed, not the instructions).
The agent is new and has no production predecessor to mirror against; in this case a canary rollout to a small percentage of users is more appropriate.
The agent's cost makes doubling traffic economically untenable and you cannot afford even 5% sampling for a statistically meaningful period.
The agent's tool calls are inherently non-idempotent and cannot be safely mocked, making true isolation impossible.
How to Implement
Step 1: Deploy the shadow backend.
Deploy the new agent as a separate service with its own endpoint. Tag all resources it creates with
env=dark-launch
. Configure the shadow backend's LLM API key with a separate quota to prevent it from consuming production rate limits.
Step 2: Configure the routing layer.
Add a mirroring rule at whichever layer you chose. For nginx, add a
mirror
block. For Istio, add a
mirror
field to your VirtualService. For an API gateway, add a shadow integration.
Step 3: Attach a shadow trace context.
Every mirrored request must carry a header (e.g.,
X-Dark-Launch: true
and
X-Shadow-Trace-Id: <production-trace-id>-shadow
) that tells the shadow backend it is in dark-launch mode and links the shadow run to the production run.
Step 4: Enforce tool isolation.
The shadow agent must read the
X-Dark-Launch
header (or an equivalent environment flag set at startup) and operate in read-only mode for all tools. Write operations must be intercepted and either dropped or redirected to a shadow store.
Step 5: Set up comparison logging.

Both the production agent and the shadow agent write their final outputs to a comparison log with the shared trace ID. A downstream pipeline joins the two records on trace ID and computes divergence metrics: exact match rate, semantic similarity, tool call count diff, cost diff, latency diff.
Step 6: Define exit criteria before you start.

Agree up front on what the data must show before you migrate traffic. Typical criteria: shadow quality must be within 2% of production quality on your evaluation rubric; shadow p95 latency must be no more than 20% above production; shadow error rate must be below 1%; the window must span at least two weeks to capture weekly traffic patterns.
Examples
The following examples show concrete routing configurations for each infrastructure layer.
Nginx mirror directive
— the simplest form of traffic mirroring. The
mirror
block fires an internal sub-request to the shadow upstream for every request that matches the location block. The sub-request is asynchronous with respect to the client response.
Istio VirtualService mirroring
— when the agent is deployed in a Kubernetes cluster managed by Istio, the service mesh handles mirroring without touching application code. The
mirror
field routes a copy of matched traffic to the shadow destination;
mirrorPercentage
caps how much of the traffic is mirrored (here 10%).

# istio-virtual-service.yaml
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: agent-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  hosts:
    - agent-service
  http:
    - match:
        - uri:
            prefix: /v1/agent/
      route:
        - destination:
            host: agent-service
            subset: v1-stable          # production
          weight: 100
      mirror:
        host: agent-shadow-service     # shadow deployment
        subset: v2-candidate
      mirrorPercentage:
        value: 10.0                    # mirror 10% of traffic
Feature flag + async dual dispatch

— an application-level approach that works in any language. The feature flag evaluation is synchronous; the shadow dispatch is fire-and-forget on a thread/task pool. This pattern is useful when the routing layer cannot easily be configured for mirroring.

import asyncio
import httpx
from flagsmith import Flagsmith

flagsmith = Flagsmith(environment_key="srv-env-key")

async def handle_chat(request: dict, user_id: str) -> dict:
    flags = flagsmith.get_identity_flags(user_id)
    dark_launch_enabled = flags.is_feature_enabled("dark_launch_v2_agent")

    # Always call production — this is on the critical path
    prod_response = await call_agent("https://agent-prod.internal", request)

    if dark_launch_enabled:
        # Fire shadow request as a background task — caller never waits for it
        asyncio.create_task(
            call_shadow_agent(request, user_id, prod_response)
        )

    return prod_response

async def call_agent(base_url: str, request: dict) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        resp = await client.post(f"{base_url}/v1/agent/chat", json=request)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()

async def call_shadow_agent(
    request: dict, user_id: str, prod_response: dict
) -> None:
    try:
        shadow_headers = {
            "X-Dark-Launch": "true",
            "X-Shadow-User-Id": user_id,
        }
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0) as client:
            resp = await client.post(
                "https://agent-shadow.internal/v1/agent/chat",
                json=request,
                headers=shadow_headers,
            )
            shadow_response = resp.json()
    except Exception as exc:
        shadow_response = {"error": str(exc)}

    # Log both responses for offline comparison
    await write_comparison_log(
        user_id=user_id,
        request=request,
        prod=prod_response,
        shadow=shadow_response,
    )

async def write_comparison_log(**kwargs) -> None:
    # Write to your analytics pipeline (BigQuery, S3, Kafka, etc.)
    ...
Real-World Example

Consider a customer support agent that handles billing inquiries. The production system uses an older model and a simple tool set (lookup_account, lookup_invoice). The new system uses a more capable model and adds a suggest_credit tool that the agent can invoke when it detects certain complaint patterns.

The engineering team is concerned about two things: (1) whether the new model's reasoning is more accurate, and (2) whether the suggest_credit tool will be invoked too aggressively, creating financial liability. A dark launch is the right approach because:

The suggest_credit tool can be mocked in shadow mode so it never actually creates a credit — the shadow agent calls the mock, receives a plausible fake response (e.g., credit_id: mock-12345), and the comparison log records that the shadow agent would have issued a credit for this conversation.
The comparison log lets the team analyse: how often did the shadow agent invoke suggest_credit? On what types of conversations? Were those the right conversations?
Latency and token cost are profiled against real billing inquiry traffic, which tends to be complex and context-heavy — much harder to simulate in staging.

After two weeks of dark launch data, the team discovers that the new agent invokes suggest_credit on 3% of sessions, mostly legitimately, but 0.4% of invocations would have been erroneous. They update the tool's guard conditions, run another dark launch cycle, and confirm the error rate drops to 0.05% before migrating the 3% of sessions to live traffic.
Common Mistakes
Allowing the shadow agent to write to production databases.

This is the most dangerous mistake. The shadow agent must operate in read-only mode for all persistent stores. Even a seemingly harmless write (e.g., logging a chat turn to the user's conversation history) can corrupt the production state visible to the user.
Sharing a rate limit with the production LLM API key.

Shadow requests consume tokens from the same quota as production requests. Under high traffic, the shadow agent can exhaust the quota and cause production failures. Always use a separate API key with its own quota for shadow traffic.
Mirroring 100% of traffic from the start.
Starting at 100% doubles your LLM costs immediately and generates more shadow data than you can analyse. Start at 1-5%, let the comparison pipeline process the data, then increase.
Not attaching a shared trace ID.
Without a shared identifier linking the production run and the shadow run, you cannot join the two records for comparison. The shadow data becomes useless.
Forgetting to propagate user context to the shadow agent.

Many agent tools personalise their behaviour based on user attributes (account tier, locale, permissions). If the shadow agent does not receive the same user context, it will behave differently for reasons unrelated to the code change under test.
Using synchronous shadow dispatch on the critical path.
If the shadow agent is called synchronously before returning the production response, the shadow agent's latency adds directly to the user's response time. Shadow calls must always be asynchronous.
Not setting exit criteria before starting.
Without pre-agreed exit criteria, dark launches run indefinitely and the data is never acted on. Agree on the success metrics and time window before you enable mirroring.
Best Practices
Start with internal users.

Before mirroring anonymous production traffic, route internal employee traffic to the shadow agent in full (non-dark) mode. Internal users can give qualitative feedback and can tolerate occasional failures.
Use a separate dark-launch LLM API key with a dedicated spend limit.
Tag all dark-launch API calls with a metadata label so you can break down costs by launch. Set a daily spend cap on the dark-launch key.
Build a comparison dashboard before you enable mirroring.

If you enable mirroring without a way to look at the data, you are generating noise. The dashboard should show: match rate by request type, latency delta (shadow minus production), cost per request, tool call divergence, and error rate.
Tag shadow spans in your distributed tracing system.
Add span attributes
dark_launch=true
and
shadow_experiment_id=<id>
to all spans generated by the shadow agent. This lets you filter shadow traffic out of production dashboards and into dedicated shadow dashboards.
Mock all write tools by default; enable selectively for specific tools after audit.

Do not trust tool authors to mark their tools as write-safe. Assume every tool writes unless explicitly verified. A mock that returns a plausible fake response is safer than a tool that silently succeeds without writing.
Run the dark launch for at least two full business cycles.
Traffic patterns vary by day of week and time of day. Two weeks is a minimum for most consumer-facing applications; four weeks if there is significant seasonal variation.
Automate the divergence analysis.

Reviewing shadow vs. production outputs manually at scale is impractical. Build an LLM-based evaluator that scores each pair on the dimensions that matter (accuracy, completeness, tone, tool call efficiency) and surfaces statistically significant regressions.
Performance and Scalability

The shadow backend must be sized to handle the mirrored traffic volume. The key difference from production sizing is that shadow traffic is lower priority: latency requirements are relaxed (users never wait for shadow), but the shadow backend must not starve. Common sizing choices:
If mirroring 10% of production traffic, size the shadow backend at 15% of production capacity (extra buffer for bursty arrival).
Shadow LLM API quota should be set to the mirroring percentage of production quota plus 20% overhead.

Shadow agent timeouts should be set generously — 2x to 3x the production agent timeout — because shadow latency does not affect users and you want the shadow agent to complete its reasoning even when it is slow.
Cost management is the dominant scalability concern. A shadow agent that uses a more capable (and expensive) model than production will quickly accumulate significant spend. Several strategies:
Percentage-based sampling.
Mirror only N% of requests. Start at 1%, increase to 5% once the pipeline is validated, cap at 10-20% unless you have a specific reason to go higher.
Request-type filtering.
Only mirror requests of the types you care about most. If you are validating a new reasoning capability, mirror only requests classified as multi-step reasoning; skip simple lookups.
Cohort-based capping.
Assign each user a cohort bucket deterministically (by hash of user ID). Mirror only the cohorts currently in the dark-launch experiment, not all users.
Time-windowed sampling.
Run the dark launch during business hours only, when your team can monitor dashboards, and suspend it overnight.

Security and Reliability
Dark launch routing introduces several security and reliability considerations that do not exist in standard deployments:
PII in shadow logs.

Shadow responses are logged for comparison. If the shadow agent processes user queries that contain PII (names, emails, account numbers), that PII will appear in the comparison log. Apply PII scrubbing (pseudonymisation or redaction) before writing to the comparison store. The shadow log should not retain more data than necessary and should be subject to the same retention policies as production logs.
Preventing side effects from shadow tool calls.
This is the most critical reliability concern. Every tool exposed to the shadow agent must be audited for write-side effects. Implement a wrapper layer:

import functools
from typing import Any, Callable

def read_only_guard(tool_fn: Callable) -> Callable:
    """
    Wraps a tool so it is a no-op in dark launch mode.
    Returns a plausible mock response instead of executing.
    """
    @functools.wraps(tool_fn)
    async def wrapper(*args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
        import os
        if os.environ.get("DARK_LAUNCH_MODE") == "true":
            # Return a safe mock response without executing the real tool
            return {
                "status": "mock",
                "dark_launch": True,
                "tool": tool_fn.__name__,
                "args": args,
                "kwargs": kwargs,
            }
        return await tool_fn(*args, **kwargs)
    return wrapper

# Usage:
@read_only_guard
async def send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -> dict:
    # Real implementation — only runs outside dark launch mode
    ...

@read_only_guard
async def update_account(account_id: str, changes: dict) -> dict:
    ...
Reliability isolation.

The shadow backend must not share database connection pools, caches, or thread pools with the production backend. A shadow agent that consumes all connections in a shared pool will starve production. Deploy shadow as a completely separate service, not a sidecar of the same process.
Network security.

The shadow backend endpoint should not be publicly accessible. It should only receive traffic from the mirroring proxy, filtered by source IP or mTLS. An attacker who discovers the shadow endpoint could invoke the new agent directly (without the read-only guard that only the shadow path enforces), potentially bypassing controls.
Mental Model

Think of dark launch traffic routing as a flight simulator that uses real aerodynamics. In a flight simulator you can practice landing in a thunderstorm without any risk to passengers. The aerodynamics — the forces acting on the aircraft — are real (or as close to real as the simulator can model). But the outcome of a crash is logged data, not a real crash.

Dark launch is the same idea applied to agentic AI: the inputs are real (copied from live user traffic), the agent's reasoning is real (it runs the full agent loop, calls tools, and produces an output), but the outcome — the response delivered to the user — is still controlled by the proven production system. Failures, regressions, and unexpected tool call sequences are logged data, not incidents.

A corollary: just as a flight simulator cannot fully substitute for actual flight (the simulator's aerodynamics model has limits), a dark launch cannot fully substitute for a canary. A small percentage of real users exposed to the new agent provides feedback that even perfect dark launch logging cannot capture: subjective satisfaction, re-engagement rate, support ticket creation. Dark launch is the step that precedes canary; canary is the step that precedes full rollout.

Another useful frame: the dark launch is a query-level integration test that runs continuously against your production traffic distribution. Unlike a scheduled integration test suite that runs a fixed set of scenarios, the dark launch discovers scenarios you did not think to write. It is adversarial not because anyone is trying to break the system, but because real users are creative in ways that test engineers are not.
Key Takeaways

Dark launch routing sends real production traffic to a shadow agent without exposing the shadow agent's responses to users. The production response is always served; the shadow response is logged for comparison.
The pattern is distinct from canary (traffic splitting): in a canary, some users see the new system; in a dark launch, no users see the new system.
For agentic AI, dark launch is essential because agent behaviour depends on the live traffic distribution in ways that staging environments cannot reproduce.

The three primary routing patterns are: header-based (route specific user segments), percentage-based sampling (hash-deterministic fraction of all requests), and cohort-based (sticky assignment by user ID hash for session consistency).
Infrastructure choices include nginx mirror directive, Istio VirtualService mirror, API gateway shadow stages, and application-level async dual dispatch via feature flags.
The shadow agent must be completely isolated from production: separate LLM quota, separate database connections, separate rate limits, and separate observability namespace.
All tools that write state must be mocked in shadow mode. A read-only guard wrapper is the safest implementation pattern.
Use a shared trace ID to link production and shadow records so you can run comparison analysis.
Start at 1-5% mirroring to control costs. Increase gradually as the comparison pipeline matures.
Define exit criteria (quality delta, latency delta, error rate, time window, stakeholder sign-off) before enabling mirroring. Without them, dark launches run indefinitely without driving a decision.
For multi-turn agentic sessions, cohort-based routing with sticky session assignment is the correct pattern. Mirroring individual turns without session continuity produces incoherent shadow runs.
